Initial diagnosis includes obtaining a history of the disease and its progression, physical examination with neurologic testing, and EMG and nerve conduction studies. Based on EMG studies, a decision can be made as to whether the disorder is axonal or demyelinating. Laboratory tests are recommended by the American Academy of Neurology for each of these categories. Serum glucose and A1c

Serum protein electrophoresis and immunofixation

B 12  level and methylmalonic acid

Anti-nuclear antibodies

Sedimentation rate

Rapid plasma reagin

Urine/blood for heavy metals

Urine/blood for porphyrins

Rheumatoid factor (RF)

Sjögren syndrome testing (anti-Ro, anti-La antibodies)

Lyme testing

HIV

Anti-Hu

Hepatitis screen (for types B and C)

Serum and urine protein electrophoresis and immuno-electrophoresis

Lumbar puncture

Hepatitis screen (for types B and C)

Anti–myelin-associated glycoprotein (MAG) testing (in patients with predominantly sensory symptoms)

Anti-GM1 test (in patients with predominantly motor symptoms)

HIV

Genetic testing for Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease

The CSF is usually normal; however, in approximately 70% of patients with diabetic neuropathy, the CSF protein is increased to >200 mg/dL. In inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathies, increase in CSF protein with minimal elevation in CSF white cells (albuminocytologic dissociation). In some cases of chronic uremia, the CSF protein is 50–200 mg/dL. In collagen vascular disease (polyarteritis nodosa has nerve involvement in 10% of patients), the CSF is usually normal. In neoplasms (leukemia, multiple myeloma, carcinoma), the CSF protein is often increased and may be associated with an occult primary neoplastic lesion outside the CNS. In alcoholism, the CSF is usually normal.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text:
Leprosy

Diphtheria: CSF protein is 50–200 mg/dL. Epstein-Barr virus (EBV; mononucleosis associated: CSF shows increased protein and up to several hundred mononuclear cells)

Serum and urine toxicology (lead, arsenic, etc.)

Blood tests for vitamin deficiencies, pregnancy, and porphyria

Nerve biopsy may be useful in determining the underlying cause of the neuropathy in cases that are difficult to differentiate between axonal and demyelinating etiologies. Nerve biopsy may also help to diagnose amyloidosis, leprosy, vasculitis, and sarcoidosis. Skin biopsy may be helpful in disorders that affect small unmyelinated nerve fibers, such as in pain, numbness, and paresthesias. Diabetic polyneuropathy is primarily a symmetrical, ascending neuropathy affecting the distal lower extremities and the hands. There is loss of vibratory sensation; impairment of pain, light touch, and temperature sensation; and decreased ankle reflexes. Patients with diabetes may present with a number of different neuropathies including symmetric polyneuropathy, autonomic neuropathy, radiculopathies, mononeuropathies, and mononeuropathy multiplex. Testing focuses on exclusion of other etiologies including metabolic disorders such as uremia, folic acid deficiency, hypothyroidism, and acute intermittent porphyria. Other entities in the differential should include alcohol, heavy metal toxicity, and exposure to hydrocarbons. Collagen vascular diseases such as periarteritis nodosa and lupus may also cause symmetric polyneuropathy. Also in the differential is infection with leprosy or inflammatory disorders such as sarcoidosis. Rare disorders including paraneoplastic syndromes, hematologic malignancy, amyloidosis, and hereditary neuropathies may also be considered. In a patient with known diabetes, the diagnosis is based on clinical examination using one of a number of testing tools. When the presentation is atypical electrodiagnostic, testing may be helpful. Laboratory testing should include screening to rule out vitamin B 12  deficiency, hypothyroidism, and uremia. Neuropathies of the cranial nerves are most commonly due to local compression of the nerve by trauma, infection, or tumor, vascular and collagen vascular disorders, and some metabolic diseases.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text:
Laboratory findings may be helpful to determine the underlying etiology:

Peripheral blood for glucose, HgbA1c, BUN, creatinine, AST, and ALT may reveal a metabolic disorder (DM, renal failure, chronic liver disease, myxedema, and porphyria). Serology and/or culture may be helpful in identification of infection (herpes zoster, benign polyneuritis associated with cervical lymph node TB, or Lyme disease). Tissue biopsy of the nerve or adjacent soft tissues may diagnose sarcoidosis and tumors (meningioma, neurofibroma, carcinoma, cholesteatoma, chordoma). Imaging studies are most useful for the detection of trauma and aneurysms. Mononeuropathy is defined as the focal dysfunction of a single nerve and may be due to trauma or compression such as carpal tunnel syndrome. Mononeuropathy multiplex is the involvement of several noncontiguous nerves. Patients present with various symptoms such as pain, paresthesias, or weakness relating to the nerve that is involved. Mononeuropathy may be due to a systemic vasculitic process that affects the vasa vasorum resulting in multiple infarcts. Other causes of mononeuropathy include

DM

Infections (e.g., HIV, diphtheria, herpes zoster, leprosy)

Sarcoidosis

Polyarteritis nodosa

Tumor (leukemia, lymphoma, carcinomas)

Trauma

Serum sickness

Bell palsy

Idiopathic

Drugs, toxic substances

Chronic renal failure

Thyroid disorders

The diagnosis of a mononeuropathy is based on history, neurologic examinations over time evaluation of progression, electrodiagnostic studies, somatosensory potentials, and neuroimaging (MRI). Fasting glucose and glycohemoglobin in patients with possible diabetic amyotrophy, idiopathic radiculopathy, or polyneuropathy

Lyme titers in patients with polyradiculopathy, especially in endemic areas

Genetic tests for hereditary neuropathy with predisposition to pressure palsy for patients with multiple mononeuropathies (usually affecting at least two to three extremities) and Chédiak-Higashi syndrome

Lumbar puncture: Evaluation of CSF is warranted in patients with unusual presentations. CSF should be examined for evidence of inflammation, elevated CSF protein, and serologic testing for Lyme disease, syphilis, and CMV. Cytologic evaluation for tumor cells may be warranted. Facial palsy is the sudden loss of function of cranial nerve VII resulting in facial paralysis and may be due to a number of etiologies, the most common being herpes simplex viral infection. Bell palsy is facial nerve paralysis of unknown etiology, but may be due to reactivation of herpes simplex. Patients with Bell palsy typically present with the sudden onset (usually over hours to days) of unilateral facial paralysis and constitute approximately 50% of patients with facial nerve palsy. Facial palsy may be due to viruses in addition to HSV: herpes zoster, CMV, EBV, adenovirus, rubella virus, mumps, influenza B, HIV, and coxsackie virus; bacteria including Lyme, syphilis, leprosy, diphtheria, catscratch disease,  Mycoplasma pneumoniae , and nonspecific local inflammation including otitis media; and parasitic infections such as malaria. Granulomatous disease such as sarcoidosis should be considered, especially in patients with bilateral facial palsy.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text:
Trauma, tumor (acoustic neuromas [see  eBook Figure 6-11 ], tumors invading the temporal bone), cholesteatoma, and Paget disease of the bone should be suspected if the onset of facial palsy is gradual. These can be diagnosed on imaging. A:  Gross cross section of acoustic neuroma. B:  Microscopic low-power view of neurilemoma. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Drug reaction, particularly to dental injections, may cause local facial neuropathy, diagnosed on history. Postvaccinal effect and GBS may cause bilateral facial palsy. Testing should be designed to rule out causes of underlying diseases, serology for herpes simplex, HIV, and other viruses,  Borrelia ,  Ehrlichia , and other agents as appropriate by history. If collagen vascular disease is suspected, an ANA test may be of help. Bell palsy may occasionally present with a slight increase in cells in the CSF. Bitemporal hemianopsia is the loss of vision in the temporal fields due to a mass lesion causing compression of the optic chiasm. Patients present with decreased vision in the temporal fields. The most common cause is pituitary adenoma (see  eBook Figure 6-12 ), but any mass lesion may be causative, including metastatic tumor, sarcoidosis, Hand-Schüller-Christian disease, meningioma of sella (see  eBook Figure 6-13 ), craniopharyngioma (see  eBook Figure 6-14 ), and aneurysm of the circle of Willis. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

(Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

A:  Gross view of craniopharyngioma. B:  Cut surface, note gelatinous appearance. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Diagnosis is predominantly made by neuroimaging. Biopsy may help identify tumor type.

Internuclear ophthalmoplegia is an impairment of horizontal eye movement. There is weak adduction of the affected eye and abduction nystagmus of the contralateral eye. It is the result of a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus.

Patients may present with a number of causative disorders including MS (most common in younger patients, also tends to be bilateral), cerebrovascular disorders (infarction is most common in older patients), infection, trauma, and tumor.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text:
Diagnosis is based on physical findings and neuroimaging with MRI and specialized neural ophthalmologic techniques such as oculographic recording. The differential diagnosis includes oculomotor nerve palsy (see below).

Testing is directed at identifying the causative disease. Tests to rule out DM, vasculopathies, multiple sclerosis (MS), myasthenia gravis, hyperthyroidism, infection, and drug toxicities will be of help.

Oculomotor nerve palsy may result from lesions of the third cranial nerve (oculomotor nerve) anywhere along its path.

Patients present with a dilated pupil and loss of adduction, depression and elevation of the eye, and a droopy lid. The diagnosis varies by patient age, type of diplopia, and lid involvement. The most common causes include intracranial aneurysm, ischemia, severe trauma, and migraine. Ischemic diabetic third nerve palsies are the most common etiology in adults.

The differential diagnosis includes MS (may mimic pupil-sparing ophthalmoplegia) and orbital inflammation or fracture. The diagnosis rests on complete history, neurologic exam, and neuroimaging with MRI, MRA, or CTA to rule out aneurysm.

Laboratory testing can help in the diagnosis of diabetes and vasculopathies (glucose, hemoglobin A1c, sedimentation rate). Testing to exclude myasthenia gravis should be performed in younger patients.

Trigeminal neuralgia is a sudden, usually unilateral, severe, brief, stabbing, recurrent pain in the distribution of one or more branches of the fifth cranial (trigeminal) nerve.

Compression of the trigeminal nerve root, by an artery or vein, or less likely by tumor or cyst leads to demyelination and sensation of pain following an innocuous stimulus.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text:
Diagnosis is performed predominantly by neuroimaging (CT or MRI) for mass and electrophysiologic testing. Laboratory tests to rule out MS (CSF oligoclonal bands) or herpes zoster (serology). Tissue biopsy may be needed in the diagnosis of schwannoma (see  eBook Figure 6-11 ), meningioma (see  eBook Figures 6-13  and  6-15 ), and cysts. A:  Gross image of meningioma attached to the meninges. B:  Low-power microscopic H&E section of tumor. C:  Medium-power H&E section with calcifications and spindled tumor cells. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Retrobulbar neuropathy is a disorder of the optic nerve resulting in pain behind the affected eye, impaired vision, and rarely blindness. Patients may present with a number of causative disorders; however, the most common is MS (demyelinating optic neuritis [ON]). Other causes include ischemia (arteritic or nonarteritic ischemic optic neuropathy usually in elderly), infectious (West Nile virus, catscratch disease, toxoplasma,  Tuberculosis , and  Cryptococcus ), tumors (paraneoplastic disease), and medications (chloramphenicol, ethambutol, isoniazid, penicillamine, phenothiazines, phenylbutazone, quinine, and streptomycin). Postviral infectious ON may also occur. Less common causes include sarcoidosis and autoimmune diseases such as lupus, Sjögren syndrome, and Wegener granulomatosis. There are two hereditary forms of optic neuropathy: Leber hereditary optic neuropathy and Kjer disease. The diagnosis is based on the elimination of underlying disorders by history and examination including funduscopic evaluation. Neuroimaging (MRI) may help confirm the presence of acute demyelinating disease and MS. Visual-evoked responses may be helpful in determining demyelination. Laboratory testing including sedimentation rate, ANA, angiotensin-converting enzyme levels, and serologic test for Lyme disease should be obtained. Lumbar puncture is helpful to rule out MS. CSF may be normal or reveal increased protein and ≤200/μL lymphocytes. OCBs may be present. Other testing should be performed to rule out possible infectious agents, toxins, and genetic disorders based on the history of the individual patient. Autonomic neuropathy is a group of diseases or syndromes affecting the parasympathetic and/or the sympathetic nerves. It can be hereditary or acquired. A wide range of symptoms affecting many different organ systems can occur, including the cardiovascular, GI, GU, pulmonary, and neuroendocrine systems. The most common cause of autonomic neuropathy is DM (see also Polyneuropathy and the section on Autoimmune Disorders of the CNS). Disorders that may cause autonomic dysfunction include amyloidosis, GBS, hereditary neuropathies, infections (e.g., Chagas disease, HIV, botulism, diphtheria, and leprosy), toxicities including drugs (vincristine, cis-platinum, Taxol, thallium, and heavy metals), collagen vascular disease (e.g., Sjögren disease, systemic lupus, RA), porphyria, uremia, alcoholic neuropathy, hepatic disease, paraneoplastic syndromes, Lambert-Eaton syndrome, and medications (antihypertensives, tricyclics, MAO inhibitors, and dopamine agonists).

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text: Laboratory testing to determine the causative disease or toxin should be based on the presenting symptoms and history of the patient to rule out the preceding disorders. All patients with diabetes should be screened for autonomic neuropathy with a complete history and physical examination, including evaluation of heart rate, respiratory rate, and response to the Valsalva maneuver and evaluation for orthostatic hypertension.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text:
Parkinson disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder resulting from the loss of dopaminergic cells in the substantia nigra. Patients present with rest tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and gait disturbance. In the late stages, PD may result in dementia (see Dementia). The differential diagnosis includes essential tremor (ET), DLB, cortical basal degeneration, PSP, and multiple system atrophy. It must also be distinguished from secondary parkinsonism due to drugs, toxins, head trauma, infections, cerebrovascular disease, and metabolic disorders. The diagnosis is based on clinical evaluation with required specific diagnostic criteria (bradykinesia and rest tremor or rigidity), supportive criteria, and criteria that are signs of alternate pathology or absolute exclusion criteria. Approximately 10% of Parkinson's cases have been linked to a genetic cause. PD has been associated with mutations in the glucocerebrosidase gene (GBA) and alpha-synuclein gene (SNCA). Most cases are idiopathic or sporadic, and researchers believe onset is due to a combination of genetic and environmental factors. In sporadic cases, associated genetic risk factors have been described; these include mutations on chromosome 1P in the ATPase gene, which is associated with juvenile parkinsonism with dementia, and multiplication-type mutations of the alpha-synuclein gene APOE ε4 and APOE ε2 and the microtubule-associated protein tau (MAPT) H1/H1 gene, which has been implicated in more rapid onset of dementia. Seven genes that cause some form of Parkinson disease have been identified. Mutations in three known genes called SNCA (PARK1), UCHL1 (PARK 5), and LRRK2 (PARK8) and another mapped gene (PARK3) have been reported in families with dominant inheritance. Mutations in three known genes, PARK2 (PARK2), PARK7 (PARK7), and PINK1 (PARK6), have been found in affected individuals who had siblings with the condition but whose parents did not have Parkinson disease (recessive inheritance). There are no specific physiologic or blood tests to confirm the diagnosis. MRI may be performed to exclude structural abnormalities of the brain. Olfactory dysfunction is seen early in PD, and testing may help to establish the diagnosis. DaTscan (striatal dopamine transporter imaging using 123I-FP-CIT single photon emission tomography) may be helpful in distinguishing PD from other parkinsonian syndromes. At autopsy, gross sectioning of the brain stem through the substantia nigra reveals the loss of pigmentation. Microscopy demonstrates loss of neurons and Lewy bodies (see  eBook Figures 6-7  and  6-16 ). (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP) is a neurodegenerative disorder resulting in the loss of neurons and glia in the basal ganglia, brain stem, cerebral cortex, dentate nucleus, and upper spinal cord. Patients present with symptoms similar to Parkinson disease but more specifically vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, unexplained falls due to postural instability, akinesia, and cognitive dysfunction.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text: A genetic susceptibility has been suggested; there is an association with variants in MAPT, STX6, EIF2AK3, and MOBP. The diagnosis of PSP is made on clinical examination. As in PD, laboratory and imaging studies are not definitive and should be performed to rule out treatable forms of disease (encephalitis, dopaminergic drug use, tumors, and Whipple disease). Blood, urine, and CSF are normal in PSP. There may be biomarkers for PSP including a low homovanillic acid level (the catabolic product of dopamine) in the CSF and decreased levels of tau protein. Pathologic evaluation of the brain at autopsy will identify globose neurofibrillary tangles (abnormally phosphorylated tau) within neurons and glia predominantly in the basal ganglia typical for PSP and midbrain and cerebral cortical atrophy with hypopigmentation of the substantia nigra and locus ceruleus. Huntington disease (HD) is a neurodegenerative disease inherited as autosomal dominant and is caused by a repeat expansion of CAG in the huntingtin gene on chromosome 4p. The disease exhibits variable penetration based on the number of repeats but is fully penetrant when the repeat size is >40. Patients present with choreiform movements, psychiatric disorder, and dementia. The differentiation from other neurodegenerative dementias is made based on the preexistence of choreiform movements and/or psychiatric illness and from other movement disorders by the manner of abnormal movements. Other hereditary neurodegenerative diseases due to C9orf72 gene hexanucleotide expansions may require genetic testing to differentiate. The diagnosis of HD is based on a familial history, clinical assessment, and genetic testing. Genetic screening is also available for family members who wish to know their risk of disease. Testing for the CAG repeat length is now commercially available with good sensitivity and 100% specificity based on a cutoff of >36 repeats for HD. Neuroimaging is no longer recommended. Dystonia is a movement disorder with sustained muscle contractions. It may be hereditary or acquired. Patients present with twisting repetitive movements and abnormal posture, intensified by voluntary action. Classification is by age of onset, anatomic distribution, and etiology. In primary dystonia, there are no neurologic symptoms in addition to the dystonic findings, while in secondary dystonia, additional findings such as spasticity, ataxia, muscle weakness, ocular or cognitive impairment, or seizures may be present. A genetic etiology has been determined for a number of the primary and secondary dystonia disorders. The primary autosomal dominant forms are TOR1A gene in DYT1, GCH1 gene in DYT5a, THAP1 gene in DYT6, and GNAL in DYT25 dystonia. The primary autosomal recessive forms are TH gene in DYT5b and the SPR gene in dopa-responsive dystonia. The diagnosis of dystonia is predominantly made on clinical examination with special attention to evaluation of the movement disorders.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text: Genetic testing for the DYT-TOR1A dystonia gene is available for early-onset dystonia. Other tests may help to exclude secondary dystonia; these include neuroimaging with MRI or CT to evaluate the basal ganglia and CBC, electrolytes, renal and liver function tests, ANA, ceruloplasmin, serum copper, and 24-hour urinary copper to rule out Wilson disease and sedimentation rate. Tourette syndrome (TS) is an inherited neuropsychiatric disorder of unknown etiology resulting in motor and phonic tics with an onset in childhood. Patients present with sudden, repetitive movements and sounds. The disorder may be chronic or transient. It has a genetic component that is complex, and there may be a rare association with mutations in the SLITRK gene on chromosome 13q31.1 and the HDC gene on 15q21-q22; however, how they may cause symptoms is unclear. Patients with TS also frequently have comorbid conditions including attention deficit disorder, obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD), obsessive–compulsive behavior, learning disorders, and oppositional defiant disorder. The diagnosis of TS is predominantly made on the clinical examination (which is normal) and history. Standard neuroimaging is not helpful. No laboratory tests are available for the positive diagnosis of TS; however, drug testing to rule out secondary tics should be performed especially for cocaine and dopamine receptor–blocking agents. Review of a blood smear may rule out neuroacanthocytosis, which has been associated with tics. Cerebral palsy is a nonprogressive dysfunction of the cerebral motor regions resulting from perinatal/neonatal brain injury. Patients present in childhood with chorea and muscle tone abnormalities, abnormal reflexes, and coordination. The diagnosis is made primarily based on history and physical findings. Testing that may help to rule out alternative causes such as abnormal development of the brain and infarcts includes MRI, cranial ultrasound, and CT scan. EEG may also be used to rule out seizure. Laboratory testing with PT, PTT, proteins C and S, and antithrombin may rule out coagulopathy as a basis for stroke that may mimic cerebral palsy. Sydenham chorea is a sequela of acute rheumatic fever and is the most common acquired form of chorea in childhood. Patients present with chorea, hypotonia, and emotional lability 1–8 months following the infection, and symptoms may be insidious or abrupt. The diagnosis is made by clinical evaluation and EKG/echocardiogram and supported by lab data. Testing should include evaluation for group A streptococcal infection, throat culture, CRP, ESR, ASO, and anti-DNAse B titers. Lesch-Nyhan syndrome is an inherited X-linked recessive trait resulting in hyperuricemia. Patients present early on with mental retardation, delayed development, extrapyramidal motor symptoms, self-mutilating behavior, and severe gout and renal disorders. A genetic mutation is found in the hypoxanthine–guanine phosphoribosyltransferase enzyme (HPRT1) gene and results in deficient enzymatic activity.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text: A large number of mutations of this gene have been reported. HPRT enzyme activity <1.5% of normal in cells from any tissue (blood, cultured fibroblasts) is diagnostic. Molecular genetic testing with sequence analysis of the entire coding region is available for Lesch-Nyhan syndrome as both a carrier test and prenatal test. Tremor is an involuntary movement caused by alternating or synchronous contractions of antagonistic muscle groups. It is classified by the oscillation frequency, body part affected, and whether the tremor occurs at rest or with activity. Tremors occurring with activity are subdivided into simple kinetic, intention, or task-specific tremors. Tremors may be genetic, acquired (disease, medications, and toxins), or idiopathic. ET is defined as an isolated tremor with no other physiologic or psychological symptoms. It is common (up to 5% of the population) and associated with a family history of tremor in approximately half of the cases. Patients present with a tremor on exertion of the affected muscle group with a frequency of 6–12 Hz. Mental or physical stress may worsen the symptoms. Most common are tremors of the hands or arms, but the head, neck, jaw, and other body parts may be affected. Studies suggest an autosomal dominant inheritance with incomplete penetrance. The diagnosis is based on clinical features, neurologic examination, a history of factors that precipitate or relieve the tremor, and review of all medications. There are no tests specific for ET. Progressive and treatable disorders such as Parkinson disease and metabolic disorders should be ruled out. Screening laboratory tests for thyroid disease (TSH and free T4), Wilson disease, diabetes, heavy metals, drug levels (sympathomimetic drugs and stimulants), caffeine, and alcohol may rule out causes for nonessential tremor. Restless legs syndrome (RLS) is a motor disorder in which patients feel the need to move their legs to alleviate discomfort. It may be primary or secondary when associated with comorbidities. It is associated with reduced CNS iron stores (decreased CSF ferritin). No definite gene mutation has been identified although many have been explored and a family history is present in up to 60% of cases. Patients present with an irresistible urge to move, most commonly when at rest or trying to sleep, because of unpleasant sensations in the legs or other body parts. The diagnosis of RLS is made on clinical evaluation and history and exclusion of other etiologies including iron studies, BUN, and creatinine. RLS may be associated with a number of medical disorders including iron deficiency, renal disease, diabetes, MS, Parkinson disease, pregnancy, rheumatic disease, and venous insufficiency. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that may be familial resulting in muscle weakness and death. It is the most common form of motor neuron disease.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
text:
Patients with ALS present with upper and lower motor neuron dysfunction beginning in the cranial/bulbar, cervical, thoracic, or lumbosacral regions. There is steady progression of the disease over the years, spreading to the other regions, resulting in weight loss and muscle wasting. Familial ALS accounts for 5–10% of all ALS cases (see  eBook Figure 6-17 ). A:  Hand of patient with ALS showing muscle wasting. B:  Cross section of the spinal cord, H&E. C:  Degeneration of the corticospinal tract identified on Luxol fast blue stain. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Diagnosis is made on clinical history and examination. EMG and neuroimaging should exclude other possible etiologies of the upper and/or lower motor neuron abnormalities.

Creatine kinase may be elevated up to 1,000 U/L due to denervation.

Evaluation of the CSF may rule out Lyme disease, HIV infection, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, malignancy, and paraneoplastic syndromes secondary to lymphoma or breast cancer.

Familial ALS genetic testing is commercially available. Mutations occur in the SOD1, TARDBP, FUS, FIG4, ANG, Alsin (ALS2), VAPB, OPTN, and SETX genes (see  Chapter 12 , Hereditary and Genetic Diseases).

Muscle biopsy may rule out myopathy. In ALS, there is chronic denervation and reinnervation.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Autoimmune Disorders of the CNS — Definition (part 71)
text:
Primary autoimmune autonomic failure (also known as autoimmune autonomic ganglionopathy, acute panautonomic neuropathy, or acute pandysautonomia) is an autoimmune disorder due to antiganglionic nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) antibodies causing dysfunction of efferent sympathetic and parasympathetic pathways. Patients present with orthostatic hypotension, anhidrosis, decreased production of saliva and tears, erectile dysfunction, and impaired bladder emptying. The disorder may be responsive to plasma exchange. The differential diagnosis should include secondary causes of autoimmune autonomic failure including DM, amyloidosis, paraneoplastic syndromes, Lambert-Eaton syndrome, botulism, syphilis, HIV infection, collagen vascular disease, and porphyria. Testing varies according to the presentation and history of autonomic symptoms. Testing should be directed to differentiate between acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathies (Parkinson disease, drug or toxin exposure, and hereditary etiologies) from primary autoimmune autonomic failure. Detection of antibodies to ganglionic nAChRs is diagnostic. There may also be decreased plasma norepinephrine levels. Glycosylated hemoglobin to test for diabetes

Anti-Hu antibody titers, which may be used to test for paraneoplastic syndromes

Anti–calcium channel antibody titers for Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS)

Serum and urine protein electrophoresis to evaluate myeloma due to amyloidosis or genetic testing to evaluate for familial amyloidosis

Rapid plasma reagin (RPR) or syphilis antibody screen

ANA levels, ESR, and other autoimmune tests (e.g., RF and Sjögren syndrome, SS-A and SS-B antibodies) to evaluate for collagen vascular disease

Urinary porphyrins and erythrocyte porphobilinogen deaminase levels for porphyria

Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is the name given to a group of heterogeneous disorders constituting the acute immune-mediated polyneuropathies. There are several variant forms including the acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (AIDP), seen in the United States and Europe. Acute motor axonal neuropathy (AMAN) and acute sensory motor axonal neuropathy (AMSAN) are usually seen in China, Japan, and Mexico. Miller-Fisher syndrome includes ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia. The usual presentation is that of an acute monophasic paralyzing illness following an infection. In 70% of cases, it is reversible, but 10% of patients die and 20% have residual defects. Dysautonomia occurs in 70% of patients, and severe autonomic dysfunction is occasionally associated with sudden death. A number of glycolipid antibodies have been found to be the underlying cause of GBS. These include antibodies against GQ1b, which is a ganglioside component of nerve seen in the Miller-Fisher variant, and antibodies to GM1, GD1a, GalNac-GD1a, and GD1b, which are associated with axonal variants. EMG and nerve conduction studies may be helpful in diagnosing GBS by revealing the predominantly demyelinating polyneuropathy of AIDP or the axonal neuropathy of AMAN or AMSAN. The CSF shows a normal cell count and increased protein (average 50–100 mg/dL). The protein increase parallels increasing clinical severity, and the increase may be prolonged. The CSF may be normal at first.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Autoimmune Disorders of the CNS — Definition (part 72)
text:
Laboratory findings due to associated disease may be present (e.g., evidence of recent infection with  Campylobacter jejuni , CMV, EBV, and  M. pneumoniae , other viral and rickettsial infections, immune disorders, DM, vasculitis, Lyme disease, exposure to toxins [e.g., lead, alcohol, arsenic, glue  n -hexane], paraneoplastic syndromes, and thiamine deficiency). MS is an inflammatory disorder of the brain and spinal cord resulting in the loss of myelin and axonal injury. It is the most common autoimmune demyelinating disease of the CNS. Patients present with distinct episodes of neurologic deficits separated in time. It is caused by an inflammatory demyelination of distinct foci of white matter that are separated in space. Women are twice as likely to be affected as men, and the disease is rare in children or in patients >50 years of age. At autopsy, histology of the brain reveals multifocal areas of demyelination with loss of oligodendrocytes and astroglial scarring (see  eBook Figure 6-18 ). Diagnosis should not be made by CSF evaluation alone unless there are  multiple clinical lesions in time  (by clinical history) and  anatomic location  (by MRI, evoked potentials, or physical examination). A:  Cross section of the brain with multiple sclerosis showing myelin loss in multiple areas. B:  Low-power view of demyelination, Luxol fast blue stain. C:  Vessel with inflammatory cuffing, Luxol fast blue. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

The diagnosis of MS is most commonly made using the McDonald criteria (revised 2017), based on clinical findings and MRI. CSF studies are not required in patients with classic symptoms or MRI findings, but may help in cases with atypical presentation. The opening pressure, glucose, and albumin levels are normal, and leukocyte count is normal in two thirds of patients. Less than 5% of patients have a WBC count >50 cells/μL. Cells are predominantly T lymphocytes. There are two significant CSF tests that are positive in MS: oligoclonal bands (OCBs) and the IgG index. OCBs found in the CSF, which are not seen in the serum, are consistent with intrathecal synthesis. A positive result may increase the risk for progression to MS in patients with an atypical or single episode of symptoms and is supportive of the diagnosis of MS. OCBs are not known to correlate with severity, duration, or course of MS, and they persist during remission. They may also be seen in a small number of patients with CNS infections, viral syndromes, or neuropathies. The immunoglobulin level, predominantly IgG, is elevated in the CSF relative to other proteins in MS. This finding is expressed as the IgG index (the normal value is <0.66). Ninety percent of MS patients have an index of >0.7.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Autoimmune Disorders of the CNS — Definition (part 73)
text: CSF IgM and IgA may also be increased but are not useful for diagnosis. CSF IgG does not correlate with duration, activity, or course of MS. It may also be increased in other inflammatory demyelinating diseases (e.g., neurosyphilis, acute GBS), other neurologic diseases, and a few normal persons. Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) is an immune-mediated chronic inflammatory disease that predominantly affects the optic nerve and spinal cord. NMOSD was originally thought to be a variant of MS, but it is now considered a distinct disease. In 2015, the consensus diagnostic criteria for NMOSD removed the distinction between NMO and NMOSD and unified the two terms as NMOSD. NMOSD typically presents with optic neuritis (ON) and myelitis. Limited forms of this disease also occur, such as isolated ON, brain stem encephalitis, and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM). In the United States, NMOSD may account for 1–2% of patients with MS diagnoses. The presence of antibodies against aquaporin-4 (AQP4) sometimes referred to as NMO-IgG in patient serum is associated with NMOSD. The presence of anti-AQP4 antibodies is also associated with poor visual outcome, and anti-AQP4 antibody titers may correlate with disease severity. Antibodies against myelin-oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) have also been detected in patients with NMOSD. Studies indicate that NMOSD patients with anti-MOG antibodies have better clinical outcomes when compared to anti–AQP4-positive patients. Additionally, anti–MOG-positive patients are less likely to have recurrent attacks and may not require long-term immunosuppressive therapy. There is no cure for NMOSD. Symptomatic treatments for patients with NMOSD are similar to those used to treat MS patients. Early initiation of immunotherapy is recommended for NMOSD. Azathioprine and rituximab are the most commonly prescribed first-line therapies, while other treatment options include mycophenolate mofetil, intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), mitoxantrone, cyclophosphamide, interferon-beta/glatiramer acetate, methotrexate, natalizumab, and fingolimod. Early diagnosis of NMOSD by testing for anti-AQP4 or anti-MOG antibodies can facilitate early initiation of therapy.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Neoplastic Disorders of the CNS — Tuberous Sclerosis (part 74)
text:
Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) is an autosomal dominant multisystem disorder characterized by hamartomas in multiple organ systems, including the brain, skin, heart, kidneys, and lung.

CNS manifestations include epilepsy, learning difficulties, behavioral problems, and autism. Skin lesions include melanotic macules, facial angiofibromas, and patches of connective tissue nevi. There is a wide clinical spectrum, and some patients may have minimal symptoms with no neurologic disability.

Tuberous sclerosis-2 is caused by a mutation in the TSC2 gene on chromosome 16p13. Approximately 10–30% of cases of tuberous sclerosis are due to mutations in the TSC1 (hamartin; LAM) gene located at 9q34.13. The frequency of cases due to mutations in the TSC2 gene is consistently higher, and TSC2 mutations are associated with more severe disease.

Neurofibromatosis type I is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by café au lait spots, Lisch nodules in the eye, and fibromatous tumors of the skin.

Variable numbers of hyperpigmented café au lait spots usually develop in the first years of life, but may be present at birth, and are often the first apparent feature of NF1. The quantity and size of these macules have not been linked to disease severity, and they show no tendency to malignant degeneration. The presence of six or more café au lait macules with diameter 0.5 cm before puberty or 1.5 cm after puberty is a diagnostic feature. Individuals with the disorder have increased susceptibility to the development of benign and malignant tumors.

NF1 is sometimes referred to as “peripheral neurofibromatosis.” The worldwide incidence of NF1 is 1 in 2,500 to 1 in 3,000 individuals. In NF1, malignant neoplasms or benign CNS tumors occurred in 45% of the probands. Type II neurofibromatosis (NF2; 101000) is a genetically distinct disorder caused by mutation in the gene encoding merlin (NF2; 607379) on chromosome 22q12.

NF2, sometimes known as “central neurofibromatosis,” is characterized by bilateral acoustic neuroma and meningioma, but few skin lesions or neurofibromas. Some patients with homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations in mismatch repair genes (see, e.g., MLH1; 120436 and MSH2; 609309) have a phenotype characterized by early-onset malignancies and mild features of NF1, especially café au lait spots; this is known as the mismatch repair cancer syndrome.

Brain tumors are a diverse group of neoplasms, both malignant and benign, with varying growth rates and symptoms. A large number of mass lesions within the intracranial vault are metastatic, arising from malignancies in other organs, or hematologic (leukemia or lymphoma).

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Neoplastic Disorders of the CNS — Tuberous Sclerosis (part 75)
text:
Patients may present with headache, seizure, nausea and vomiting, syncope, cognitive dysfunction, weakness, loss of sensation, and aphasia. Suspicion for intracranial neoplasm should be followed by neurologic testing and neuroimaging, MRI with contrast or CT if emergent. Definitive diagnosis of an intracranial mass lesion is made on biopsy or cytology of the CSF. Frozen section or smear evaluation of tumoral tissue can be made in the operating room, which will allow the neurosurgeon to proceed with definitive resection if possible (see  eBook Figure 6-19 ).

A:  Cut surface of the brain with diffuse astrocytoma.  B:  Astrocytoma, H&E.  C:  Cut surface of the brain with glioblastoma multiforme.  D:  Microscopic GBM with hemorrhage, H&E.  E:  Gross surface of the cortex with metastatic melanoma.  F:  Cut surface of the brain with metastatic lung cancer.

Evaluation of the CSF may help in the diagnosis. The CSF is usually clear, but it may occasionally reveal xanthochromia or be frankly bloody if there is hemorrhage into the tumor. The WBC count may be increased ≤150 cells/μL in up to 75% of patients and normal in others. Protein is usually increased. Protein is particularly increased with meningioma of the olfactory groove and with acoustic neuroma.

Tumor cells may be demonstrable in up to 40% of patients with all types of solid tumors, but failure to find malignant cells does not exclude neoplasm. Atypical WBCs may be seen in leukemia or lymphoma. Tumor antigens/markers may indicate the source of some metastatic tumors. Glucose may be decreased if cells are present. OCBs may be present in patients with tumor and are nonspecific.

Brain stem gliomas and “diencephalic syndrome,” which are characteristically found in childhood, are usually associated with normal CSF.

This tumor arises from the jugular and tympanic parasympathetic ganglia within the ear and is the most common tumor of the middle ear. It is a slow-growing, vascular tumor, with blood supply from the external and/or internal carotid artery. It has the ability to secrete catecholamines.

The sporadic form is more common in women and the hereditary form is equally distributed in men and women. The tumor may result in hearing loss with pulsing/ringing in the ear, dizziness, and ear pain. Paragangliomas are a component of multiple endocrine neoplasia types 2A and 2B, neurofibromatosis type 1, von Hippel-Lindau, and Carney-Stratakis dyad. Nonsyndromic familial forms of paraganglioma are noted to have mutations in the SDH gene for which screening is available.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Neoplastic Disorders of the CNS — Tuberous Sclerosis (part 76)
text:
The diagnosis is made by neurophysiologic testing and CT or MRI. Blood and urine tests for endocrine workup (urinary and/or plasma fractionated metanephrines and catecholamines) should be performed. Evaluation of the CSF may reveal an increase in protein. Asymptomatic carriers of an SDH mutation may benefit from radiologic screening for early detection.

Intracranial hemorrhage is the principal cause of death in leukemia (may be intracerebral, subarachnoid, or subdural). It is more frequent when the WBC count is >100,000/μL and with rapid increases in the WBC count, especially in blast crises. The platelet count is frequently decreased. Often, there is evidence of bleeding elsewhere. At autopsy, the tumor may be identified in the arachnoid, meninges, and perivascular regions (see  eBook Figure 6-20 ).

A:  Low-power H&E section of meningeal leukemia,  B:  Perivascular cuffing of leukemia.  C:  High-power H&E section of acute leukemia.

Evaluation of the CSF may be diagnostic. There may be intracranial hemorrhage and infiltration of leukemic cells (blasts) into the meninges and fluid. The CNS is involved in 5% of patients with ALL at diagnosis and is the major site of relapse. PCR is used to detect minimal residual cells that are not recognized morphologically. Involvement of the CSF by AML is less common than ALL and CLL, and plasmacytoid leukemias are very rare.

The CSF may show an increased opening pressure and protein level. The glucose may be decreased to <50% of the blood level. Abnormal cells may be identified by cytochemical, immunohistochemical, immunofluorescent, or flow cytometric techniques to help diagnose leukemia.

Evaluation of the CSF may also help in identifying complicating meningeal infection (e.g., various bacteria, opportunistic fungi).

Cytologic evaluation of the CSF may provide sufficient diagnostic material to avoid brain biopsy in some patients. The meninges are involved in <30% of patients with malignant lymphoma. Involvement is most prevalent in diffuse large cell (“histiocytic”), lymphoblastic, and immunoblastic lymphoma. CSF often reveals an elevated protein and a lymphocyte-predominant pleocytosis. The glucose level is usually normal but may be decreased if there is leptomeningeal disease. Abnormal cells found in the CSF may be differentiated by immunohistochemistry, immunofluorescence, or flow cytometry. PCR immunoglobulin heavy chain rearrangement may also be used for identification of clonality. The demonstration of neoplastic lymphocytes in the CSF on cytologic evaluation or flow cytometry is sufficient for the diagnosis of CNS lymphoma. Stereotactic needle biopsy is performed if the CSF is nondiagnostic.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Neoplastic Disorders of the CNS — Tuberous Sclerosis (part 77)
text:
Spinal cord tumors occur within the cord parenchyma or membranes adjacent to the cord. They may be primary or metastatic. Primary spinal cord tumors account for 2–4% of all primary CNS tumors. Extradural tumors are usually metastatic and can cause spinal cord compression. Tumors arising within the dura, outside of the spinal cord, are termed intradural–extramedullary and comprise the nerve sheath tumors and meningiomas. Tumors arising within the spinal cord itself are called intramedullary tumors, predominantly gliomas (astrocytomas or ependymomas).

Patients present with progressive symptoms that vary based on the location of the compression of the cord and spinal nerve roots. Symptoms range from pain to loss of sensation or motor function and decreased sensitivity to heat or cold and bowel or bladder dysfunction.

Evaluation of the CSF reveals increased protein. The level may be very high and is associated with xanthochromia when there is a block of the subarachnoid space. The protein concentration is higher with complete block in cord tumors located at lower levels. Tumor cells may be demonstrable. Definitive diagnosis is made on tissue evaluation at biopsy (see  eBook Figure 6-21 ).

A:  Low-power H&E section of ependymoma.  B:  High-power view of tumor cords and rosettes.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Congenital Disorders of the CNS — Definition (part 78)
text:
Neural tube defects (NTD) are due to a failure of the closure of the embryonic neural tube. They are second only to cardiac malformations as the cause of congenital anomalies. The supplementation of folic acid for all pregnant women has decreased the incidence of NTD markedly in the United States. NTD range from the most severe (anencephaly) to mild spinal defects (spina bifida) (see  eBook Figure 6-22 ).

A:  Meningocele in a premature infant.  B:  Meningomyelocele in a premature infant.  C:  Anencephaly.  D and E:  Total rachischisis (complete open neural tube).

The risk factors for NTD include folic acid deficiency, certain drugs (valproic acid, carbamazepine, and methotrexate), diabetes, obesity, and hyperthermia. Genetic factors may be associated with NTD, and fetuses with NTD have a high rate of karyotypic abnormalities. Trisomy 18 is the most common chromosomal abnormality found in NTD.

Screening for NTD should be offered to all pregnant women including high-quality second trimester ultrasound and/or alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) in the maternal serum. AFP screening should occur between 15 and 20 weeks, and results are expressed as multiples of the median (MoM) for each gestational week. Values >2.0–2.5 MoM are abnormal. AFP is more reliable for open NTD, and the detection rate for anencephaly is as high as 95%. Care should be taken in the interpretation of this test as it is affected by gestational age, maternal weight, maternal diabetes, multiple gestation, race, and fetal anomalies other than NTD.

Mitochondrial disorders are a clinically heterogeneous group of diseases caused by dysfunction of the mitochondrial respiratory chain resulting from mutations in mitochondrial (mtDNA) or nuclear-encoded genes (ntDNA) required for mitochondrial function.

Mitochondrial DNA defects are transmitted by maternal inheritance. The human mtDNA is a double-stranded, circular molecule of 16 569 bp and contains 37 genes coding for two rRNAs, 22 tRNAs, and 13 polypeptides. The mtDNA-encoded polypeptides are all subunits of enzyme complexes of the oxidative phosphorylation system.

The number of nuclear candidate genes responsible for mitochondrial disorders continues to grow. Many primary mitochondrial diseases caused by mutations in the mitochondrial genome involve multiple organ systems, reflecting the dependence on oxidative phosphorylation–derived energy in a wide variety of tissues. Heteroplasmy (copy number within a cell) and the varied distribution and dosage of mutant mitochondria in tissues and organs (mitotic segregation) also play a role in phenotypic presentation.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Congenital Disorders of the CNS — Definition (part 79)
text: The wide range of clinical presentations, frequent need for invasive testing procedures (e.g., muscle biopsy), and enormous genetic heterogeneity all contribute to the diagnostic challenge. There are “classic” clinical syndromes caused by mutations in the mitochondrial genes with distinguishing pathologic and muscle biopsy features such as Alpers disease (epilepsy and liver failure; POLG gene ntDNA mutations) and mtDNA disorders, MELAS (mitochondrial myopathy, encephalopathy, lactic acidosis and stroke-like episodes), MERRF (myoclonic epilepsy with ragged-red fibers), and NARP (neuropathy, ataxia, retinitis pigmentosa). However, many patients exhibit only nonspecific features of DD or regression, further complicating arriving at accurate diagnoses.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS — Central Nervous System Trauma (part 80)
text:
Laboratory findings vary depending on the type of brain injury (e.g., contusion, laceration, subdural hemorrhage, extradural hemorrhage, subarachnoid hemorrhage). There may also be laboratory findings due to complications of the brain injury (e.g., pneumonia, meningitis). Basilar skull fracture  results in the disruption of the barriers between the sinonasal cavity and the anterior and middle cranial fossae and subsequent leak of CSF into the nasal cavity or ear (see  eBook Figure 6-23 ). This communication with the CNS can lead to infectious complications, resulting in morbidity and mortality. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Beta-2 transferrin  is produced by neuraminidase activity within the CNS; it is found in the CSF, perilymph, and aqueous humor. Immunofixation electrophoresis with anti–transferrin-precipitating antibody is performed to differentiate CSF desialated transferrin from nasal secretions. The assay has a high sensitivity and specificity. Beta-trace protein , also known as prostaglandin D synthase, has been used to diagnose CSF rhinorrhea in multiple studies, with a sensitivity of 92% and specificity of 100%. It is synthesized primarily in arachnoid cells, oligodendrocytes, and the choroid plexus, but it is also present in the testes, heart, and serum, and it is nonspecific for CSF. Prostaglandin D synthase may also be altered in renal failure, MS, cerebral infarction, and certain CNS tumors. Testing for the glucose content in rhinorrhea with the use of glucose oxidase paper is not recommended for the following reasons: reducing substances in lacrimal gland secretions and nasal mucus may cause false-positive results, and meningitis may lower the glucose level in the CSF leading to a false-negative result. Epidural hemorrhage (bleeding between the dura and skull) is a rare but serious complication of head injury. It is most commonly due to an injury of the arteries predominantly the middle meningeal artery. It is found in up to 4% of patients surviving injury and up to 15% of autopsy series (see  eBook Figure 6-24 ). (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Patients may present with a lucid interval following the head injury. Expansion of the hematoma eventually causes pressure on the brain with shift of the tissues. Symptoms include abnormalities of cranial nerve III function or weakness of the extremities on the contralateral side. Severe bleeds may progress to transtentorial or uncal herniation and death. Epidural hemorrhage may also be due to nontraumatic etiologies including infection, coagulopathy, vascular and congenital malformations, and tumors. Rarely, it may occur during hemodialysis, pregnancy, and cardiac surgery. It may also occur in the epidural space of the spinal cord following spinal procedures. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in cases where epidural hemorrhage is suspected, due to the risk of herniation. Diagnosis is primarily based on neuroimaging with unenhanced CT.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS — Central Nervous System Trauma (part 81)
text:
Differentiation of epidural versus subdural hematoma can be made on imaging as epidural hematoma will not cross suture lines and has a lens-shaped appearance. Acute subdural hematoma  is most commonly due to the shearing effect on the bridging veins between the brain and the dural sinus during trauma. Occasionally, it may also be due to arterial injury or low CSF pressure. It is a complicating factor in up to 20% of severe traumatic brain injuries (see  eBook Figure 6-25 ). (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Most patients develop coma following injury, but some have a transient “lucid interval” after the acute injury, followed by a progressive neurologic decline to coma. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated due to the risk of herniation. The diagnosis is readily made on CT of the brain with subdural hematomas appearing as crescent-shaped lesions as opposed to the lens-shaped lesion of the epidural hematoma. Chronic subdural hematoma  develops over days or weeks following minor injury that may not have been recognized by the patient. The bleed is slow and may be recurrent. Chronic subdural hematoma is more commonly seen in the elderly and may present with the insidious onset of cognitive impairment, headaches, light-headedness, apathy, somnolence, or seizures. Symptoms may only appear weeks after the initial injury and may be transient or fluctuating. Diagnosis is based on CT imaging with membranous encapsulation visualized surrounding the hematoma. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated due to the risk of herniation. CSF is usually xanthochromic, and the CSF protein content is 300– 2,000 mg/dL. Anemia is often present in infants. Stroke or cerebrovascular accident is the loss of function of an area of the brain due to vascular compromise. Stroke or ischemia of the brain may be transient or permanent. The causes include intracerebral (see  eBook Figure 6-26 ) or subarachnoid hemorrhage (see  eBook Figure 6-27 ) and thrombotic or embolic ischemia (see  eBook Figure 6-28 ). The American Heart Association data review of strokes finds that the majority of strokes are due to ischemia (87%), followed by intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) (10%) and subarachnoid hemorrhage (3%). (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

A:  Subarachnoid hemorrhage cortical surface. B:  Subarachnoid hemorrhage basilar surface. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

A:  Acute thrombotic infarction with edematous shift of the midline seen on cross section of the brain. B:  H&E section of acute infarct with intraparenchymal hemorrhage. C:  H&E of old infarct with inflammation and liquefactive necrosis. D:  Gross cut surface of the brain with resolving infarct. E:  Basilar artery atherosclerosis. F:  Cut section of basilar artery with atherosclerosis and thrombus. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

The presenting signs depend on the size and location of the infarct.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS — Central Nervous System Trauma (part 82)
text:
Risk factors include hypertension, trauma, drugs, smoking, alcoholism, atherosclerosis, and vascular malformations. The diagnosis of stroke is made on history, physical examination, and neurologic imaging (CT or MRI) to identify hemorrhagic versus thrombotic (or embolic) stroke and rule out mass lesion. If the blood pressure is normal, berry aneurysm, hemorrhage into tumor, angioma, or coagulopathy should be considered. Rapid diagnosis is important for the treatment of the patient as thrombolysis must be initiated within the first 4.5 hours after the initial event. In hemorrhagic stroke, the most common causes include ruptured berry aneurysm (45% of patients), hypertension (15% of patients), angiomatous malformations (8% of patients), and less commonly brain tumor and blood dyscrasia. Anticoagulant use is the most common cause of bleeding disorder resulting in stroke. Ischemic causes of stroke include thrombosis or embolism in 80% of patients and hypoperfusion. Blood testing at the time of suspected stroke should include CBC, PT, and PTT, thrombin time or ecarin clotting time (for patients taking a thrombin or factor Xa inhibitor), and lipid panel. A hypercoagulable panel including lupus anticoagulant (LA), anticardiolipin antibodies (ACAs), protein C, protein S, and factor V Leiden should also be obtained in cases of thrombotic stroke. Testing to rule out SLE may be indicated. Additional tests may include a fibrinogen level, ESR, serology for Lyme disease and HIV, and toxicology to rule out cocaine and other drugs. Embolism is caused by particles of debris or tissue that originate distally (from vessel walls, the heart, or tumors) and travel through the circulation resulting in the blockage of arterial blood flow to the brain. Unlike thrombosis, which involves the local vessel, local therapy in embolic stroke is only temporizing. The source of the embolic fragment must be identified and treated, or additional events may occur. There are four categories of embolic stroke: those with known cardiac source, those with a possible cardiac or aortic source, those with an arterial source, and those with an unknown source (see  eBook Figure 6-29 ). Cerebral embolism is the most common cause of stroke in the elderly. A:  Posttraumatic fat emboli in the cortex and  (B)  cerebellum. C:  Embolus in artery. D:  Vascular embolus resulting in hemorrhage of the brain stem. E:  Marantic vegetations of the cardiac valves is a common source of emboli. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Embolic stroke should be suspected if the change is sudden with maximal deficit at the onset, if the infarct is large, if there is a known cardiac or large arterial lesion, if the infarct is or becomes hemorrhagic on CT, if there are multiple lesions, and if clinical findings improve quickly. It is more common in patients with strokes of the posterior circulation.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS — Central Nervous System Trauma (part 83)
text:
Atrial fibrillation, cardiac murmurs, and enlargement are risk factors for embolus of cardiac origin. A possible cardiac source should always be considered even in young patients. If the patient is febrile, septic emboli and endocarditis should be suspected. Small vessel (lacunar) stroke is most commonly seen in patients with hypertension, DM, or polycythemia. Patent foramen ovale is also a risk factor for venous to arterial embolism. The diagnosis of embolic stroke is primarily made on clinical examination and neuroimaging (MRI and CT scan). Cardiac evaluation with EKG and echocardiography is also helpful. Doppler studies of the large vessels of the neck and aorta may show lesions in these regions. Additional imaging studies to rule out myxoma of the left atrium, fat embolism in fracture of long bones, and air embolism following neck, chest, or cardiac surgery may be indicated. Laboratory tests should be performed to try to determine any underlying disorder and should include blood cultures to rule out bacterial endocarditis, hypercoagulable panel to rule out nonbacterial thrombotic vegetations on heart valves (LA, anticardiolipin, prothrombin mutation, factor V Leiden mutation), and cardiac enzymes to rule out underlying myocardial infarction with mural thrombus. Lumbar puncture with evaluation of the CSF may reveal findings similar to cerebral thrombosis. Hemorrhagic infarction develops in one third of patients post embolism, usually producing slight xanthochromia. Some patients may have grossly bloody CSF (10,000 RBCs/μL). Septic embolism (e.g., bacterial endocarditis) may cause increased WBC (CSF WBC count ≤200/μL with variable lymphocytes and PMNs), increased RBC (CSF RBC count ≤1,000/μL), slight xanthochromia, increased protein, normal glucose, and negative culture. Intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) is defined as bleeding into the brain parenchyma and has many etiologies (see  eBook Figure 6-30 ). A:  Intracerebral hemorrhage in an infant’s brain. B:  Intracerebral hemorrhage in an adult brain consistent with hypertensive bleed in the watershed areas. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

ICH is the second most common cause of stroke following ischemic stroke. It is the cause of up to 15% of all first-time strokes, with a higher incidence in Asians, Hispanics, and African Americans. The risk for ICH is increased in patients with hypertension, amyloidosis, vascular malformations, and berry aneurysm. Risk also increases with age and alcohol and drug use. Etiologies of ICH include hypertensive vasculopathy, septic emboli, brain tumor, infection, vasculitis, bleeding disorders (including anticoagulants), and drugs such as cocaine and amphetamines. The diagnosis of intracranial hemorrhage is made by neurologic imaging (CT or MRI). Lumbar puncture reveals an increased CSF WBC count (15,000–20,000/μL), higher than in cerebral infarct (e.g., embolism, thrombosis). All patients should have a platelet count, PT, and PTT to establish bleeding potential.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS — Central Nervous System Trauma (part 84)
text:
Tests that may be helpful include an elevated ESR in vasculitis and urinalysis, which may reveal transient glycosuria or concomitant renal disease. Additional tests may be obtained to rule out causes of ICH such as leukemia, aplastic anemia, polyarteritis nodosa, SLE, and other coagulopathies. A berry or saccular aneurysm is a rounded dilation of an artery in the brain. The wall of the aneurysm is weaker than the normal vessel and therefore at risk for rupture with increased pressure (see  eBook Figure 6-31 ). The majority of subarachnoid hemorrhages are due to ruptured saccular aneurysms. A:  Aneurysm of the basilar artery. B:  Cross section of saccular aneurysm with compression of the brain stem. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Patients with subarachnoid hemorrhage due to aneurysm rupture present with sudden severe headache, nausea, vomiting, vision loss, or loss of consciousness. There may be a sentinel headache following a minor bleed that precedes rupture. The incidence of saccular aneurysms is approximately 2% among the general population. The risk of rupture varies with aneurysm size, and most occur in the 40- to 60-year-old age group, with a slight increase in women over men. African Americans have a higher incidence than do Caucasians. Risk factors for saccular aneurysm include smoking, hypertension, genetic diseases (adult-dominant polycystic kidney disease, aldosteronism, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome), family history, sympathomimetic drugs such as phenylpropanolamine and cocaine, and decreased estrogen as is seen in postmenopausal women. The diagnosis of saccular aneurysm rupture is made by identification of symptomatology and laboratory tests. CT scan will identify subarachnoid clot. Lumbar puncture reveals increased opening pressure and elevated RBC count that does not decrease between tubes 1 and 4. Bloody CSF clears by the 10th day in 40% of patients. On occasion, hemorrhage is wholly within the parenchyma, resulting in normal CSF findings. Cerebral venous and dural sinus thrombosis is the presence of clot within the sinus. Cerebral vein and dural sinus thromboses are an uncommon cause of stroke. They are more likely to occur in neonates and children than adults and more likely in women than in men. The causes of thrombosis include prothrombotic conditions in 85% of cases (oral contraception, pregnancy, malignancy), infection (e.g., otitis, mastoiditis, sinusitis, meningitis), and head injury. Genetic disorders may also be implicated, including antithrombin III deficiency, protein C and protein S deficiency, factor V Leiden mutation, and prothrombin gene mutation. Collagen vascular and inflammatory diseases (e.g., SLE, sarcoidosis, and Wegener granulomatosis), vasculitis (e.g., polyarteritis nodosa, Takayasu syndrome, dissecting aneurysm of the aorta, syphilis, meningitis; see  Chapter 5 , Cardiovascular Disorders), and hypotension (e.g., myocardial infarction, shock) are other potential causes of cerebral vein thrombosis.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS — Central Nervous System Trauma (part 85)
text:
Venous thrombosis results in elevated venous pressure followed by leakage into the surrounding parenchyma and resultant impairment of CSF reabsorption leading to increased intracranial pressure. Patients may present with headache that increases over several days, motor weakness, paresis, or seizures. Thromboses of the cerebral veins and sinuses may produce focal hemorrhagic infarcts, which can be seen on neuroimaging (see  eBook Figure 6-32 ). (Image from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

The diagnosis of cerebral vein thrombosis is made predominantly by neurologic imaging (CT or MRI). Laboratory tests are nonspecific but may give clues to the etiology. Routine hematology, coagulation (PT, PTT), and chemistry tests are recommended and give an idea of underlying cause. Evaluation of an underlying hypercoagulable state with antithrombin, proteins C and S, factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation, LA, anticardiolipin, and anti-β 2  glycoproteins may be helpful. An elevated  d -dimer may support the diagnosis but will not exclude it if negative. Lumbar puncture for evaluation of CSF to rule out infection is required. Up to 50% of patients may have CSF findings. The CSF may reveal normal or mildly increased protein to ≤100 mg/dL. The CSF cell count may be normal or ≥10 WBC/μL during the first 48 hours and rarely ≥2,000 WBC/μL transiently on the 3rd day. Red cells may be increased. Additional blood tests may be informative. An increased CRP and ESR are risk factors for development of stroke, and an increased CRP is associated with a poorer short-term prognosis. Hematologic disorders (e.g., polycythemia, sickle cell disease, thrombotic thrombocytopenia, and macroglobulinemia) may be identified (see  Chapter 11 , Hematologic Disorders). Hypertensive encephalopathy is usually associated with a blood pressure of ≥180/120 mm Hg and is an acute, life-threatening disorder presenting with signs of cerebral edema (see  eBook Figure 6-33 ). (Image from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS — Central Nervous System Trauma (part 86)
text:
Clinical symptoms are characterized by the insidious onset of headache, nausea, and vomiting. If not treated, patients progress to mental confusion, seizures, and coma. Although these symptoms differ from the abrupt onset of stroke, an MRI scan should be obtained, which may reveal edema in the parieto-occipital regions (reversible posterior leukoencephalopathy) or in the pontine region (hypertensive brain stem encephalopathy).

Findings on laboratory tests are due to changes in other organ systems and to underlying conditions such as cardiac, renal, and endocrine disorders and toxemia of pregnancy. Testing may also reveal changes that may occur due to progressive disorders following hypertensive encephalopathy such as focal ICH. The CSF frequently shows increased pressure and protein ≤100 mg/dL.

Thrombophlebitis or inflammation of the vein results from infected or septic thrombosis of the venous sinus.

Septic dural sinus thrombosis has become a rare disease since the advent of antibiotics. Patients present with headache, occasionally eye swelling or diplopia, and alterations in mental status. The diagnosis is primarily made by imaging studies; however, lumbar puncture can be supportive, differentiating periorbital cellulitis from septic cavernous sinus thrombosis.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS — Central Nervous System Trauma (part 87)
text:
Laboratory findings that may be helpful include the CBC where an elevated peripheral WBC count may suggest an acute bacterial infection or other causes of venous thromboses such as sickle cell disease, polycythemia, or dehydration. The CSF reveals inflammatory cells in 75% of cases with increased neutrophils or mononuclear cells, elevated protein, normal glucose, and a negative culture. Thirty percent of patients have a CSF finding consistent with bacterial meningitis with a positive culture. Culture of the CSF reveals  Staphylococcus aureus  in 70% of cases and is associated with facial infection or sphenoid sinusitis. MRSA is becoming more frequent. Streptococci are less commonly identified. Anaerobes may be seen in association with sinus, dental, or tonsillar infections. Fungal pathogens have also been reported. Spinal cord infarction may result from occlusion of the anterior spinal artery and the posterior spinal artery, venous occlusion, or hypoperfusion. Symptoms of paresis either unilateral or bilateral occur with infarction of the anterior spinal artery and loss of touch, proprioception, and vibratory sense with infarction of the posterior spinal artery. Venous infarction may also occur and is usually associated with vascular malformations. The differential diagnosis includes transverse myelitis, compression, and acute polyneuropathy. Diagnosis is made primarily by neuroimaging (MRI) and vascular imaging (CTA or MRA). Lumbar puncture is recommended in younger patients to rule out infectious or inflammatory etiologies including cell count, glucose, protein, gram stain, and culture. In spinal infarct, the CSF may be normal or may show mild pleocytosis with WBC < 100 and mild elevation in protein. Infectious agents such as Lyme, herpes, varicella, coxsackie, EBV, syphilis, and CMV should be ruled out with serology. Blood and urine toxicology should be performed to rule out cocaine. Additional blood tests to rule out hypercoagulable state and collagen vascular disease may be of benefit. Vasculitis is an inflammation of the vessels that may occur within the CNS, and most commonly it is due to collagen vascular disease but may also be due to infection, atherosclerosis, embolic disease, malignancy, and drugs. Primary angiitis of the CNS is a rare disorder of unknown etiology presenting predominantly in men and may occur at any age (see  eBook Figure 6-34 ). Giant cell arteritis is one of the more common forms of vasculitis. A:  Gross view of necrotizing vasculitis of the basilar artery. B:  Cut surface os brain showing punctate hemorrhage and edema from necrotizing vasculitis. C:  Cross section of vessel with marked inflammation and thrombus, H&E. D:  High-power H&E showing inflammation in the arterial wall. (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)

Patients may present with various neurologic symptoms depending on the area of the brain affected and the severity of the disease.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS — Central Nervous System Trauma (part 88)
text: In giant cell arteritis, patients present with neurologic symptoms of headache and visual disturbances. Amyloid angiopathy (see Vascular Dementia) may also occur in the CNS. The differential diagnosis of vasculitis is broad, and diagnostic testing includes clinical examination and history, neuroimaging such as MRA or CTA, and evaluation of the blood and CSF. Blood work should include a sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein, which may be elevated in collagen vascular diseases, infection, and temporal arteritis. These are normal in primary angiitis. Serologic evaluation should be carried out to rule out syphilis, Lyme disease, cat scratch disease, tuberculosis, herpes virus, hepatitis B and C virus, HIV, and cysticercosis. Rheumatologic studies should be performed to rule out collagen vascular disease (ANA, RF, antineutrophilic cytoplasmic antibody). A lumbar puncture with analysis of the CSF should be performed to rule out infection (cell count and culture), malignancy (cytology), or hemorrhage with evidence of xanthochromia. If the diagnosis cannot be made with less invasive means, a biopsy should be performed. Biopsy will help in the diagnosis of giant cell arteritis and primary angiitis of the CNS and exclude malignancy or infection. Vascular changes include loss of the internal elastic lamina and inflammatory infiltrates of the vascular wall by histiocytes, giant cells, and lymphocytes.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Affecting the CNS — Definition (part 89)
text: The paraneoplastic neurologic syndromes are a group of disorders resulting from an immunologic response directed against a normal antigen of the nervous system. These may be shared antigens with a tumor resulting in classification of the antigen as foreign by the immune system. Antibodies to intracellular neuronal proteins comprise the classical paraneoplastic antibodies such as anti-Hu, which are almost always associated with a tumor and are markers for a cytotoxic T-cell–mediated mechanism of disease. Antibodies against cell surface or synaptic proteins such as anti GABA-A receptor may occur with or without a tumor and are directly cytotoxic. Patients present with muscle weakness and autonomic dysfunction as part of one of a number of paraneoplastic syndromes: encephalomyelitis, limbic encephalitis, subacute cerebellar degeneration, LEMS, chronic gastrointestinal pseudo-obstruction, opsoclonus–myoclonus, subacute sensory neuronopathy, and dermatomyositis. Antibodies can be detected in both the serum and CSF of patients with these disorders. Patients with small cell lung carcinoma may have multiple antibodies with or without symptoms. Some patients without an underlying carcinoma may also have antibodies causing neurologic disease. Paraneoplastic symptoms and antibodies may be present years before a malignancy is diagnosed. The identification of antibodies in a patient does not necessarily predict symptomatology. In patients suspected of a classical paraneoplastic syndrome, serum will reveal the antibody. The well-characterized paraneoplastic antibodies and associated tumors include anti-Hu (small cell lung cancer [SCLC]), anti-Yo (GYN, breast), anti-Ri (SCLC, GYN, breast), anti-Tr (Hodgkin lymphoma), anti-CV2/CRMP5 (SCLC, thymoma), anti-Ma 1 and 2 (germ cell, lung, other), antiamphiphysin (breast, lung), anti-VGCC (SCLC), anti-PCA-2 (SCLC), antibipolar cells of the retina (melanoma), and antirecoverin (SCLC). In addition to evaluation of antibodies in the CSF, spinal fluid should be evaluated for malignant cells by cytology and for inflammatory changes such as pleocytosis and OCBs to rule out MS. Autoimmune dementia is a rapidly progressive dementia that is responsive to steroids. It is more common in females. The etiology is unknown. There are several types of immune-mediated encephalitis in children that may present with seizures. Children usually do not have an underlying neoplasm. The encephalitis may be associated with anti-NMDAR or other synaptic proteins such as Caspr2. Although this is a rare disorder, it is responsive to immunotherapy and therefore important to recognize. Myasthenia gravis is a disorder of neuromuscular transmission resulting from the development of autoantibodies directed against the acetylcholine receptor (AChR) or muscle-specific tyrosine kinase (MuSK). This results in the destruction of proteins in the postsynaptic membrane of the neuromuscular junction. The majority of patients presenting with myasthenia gravis have thymoma and occasionally SCLC, thyroid, or breast cancer. Patients present with fluctuating weakness of the extraocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles.

source: Wallach's Interpretation of Diagnostic Tests, 11th Edition
section_title: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Affecting the CNS — Definition (part 90)
text:
Weakness of the eyelid and extraocular muscles is present in most patients and precedes limb weakness; limb weakness alone is rare in myasthenia gravis. The differential diagnosis includes the LEMS, thyroid ophthalmopathy, ALS, botulism, and cranial nerve or brain stem pathology.

Diagnosis is made by physical examination (Tensilon or ice pack test), history, and serologic testing for AChR or MuSK antibodies. These tests are confirmatory in up to 85% of patients with generalized disease. The most sensitive test for AChR-ab is the binding antibody test by radioimmunoassay, which is highly specific for myasthenia gravis. Antibody titers do not correlate with severity of disease and correlate poorly between patients. MuSK antibodies may be detected in up to half of the patients who are negative for AChR antibodies and may correlate with disease severity.

LEMS is an autoimmune disease associated with malignancy and results from the development of antibodies to the voltage-gated calcium channel (VGCC), which interfere with the normal calcium flux required for the release of acetylcholine. It is strongly associated with SCLC but occurs less frequently than myasthenia gravis.

Patients present with symmetrical proximal muscle weakness, which starts in the lower extremities (difficulty rising from a chair), and autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth). The differential diagnosis includes myasthenia gravis, muscular dystrophy, polyneuropathies, and multiple cranial mononeuropathies.
